items.correct: Ideological incompatibility plus mutual suspicion after WWII — disputes over Eastern Europe, the atomic bomb, and broken wartime agreements.
items.misconception: Stalin started it by invading Eastern Europe after the war.
items.random_wrong: It began when the USSR shot down a US spy plane.
items.why: Students treat one side as solely responsible. Most historians now see the Cold War as a mutual breakdown — both sides acted on rational fears that the other read as aggression.

items.q: What did the Truman Doctrine commit the US to in 1947?
items.correct: Supporting free peoples resisting communist takeover — initially Greece and Turkey — formalising the policy of containment articulated by George Kennan.
items.misconception: The Truman Doctrine declared war on the Soviet Union.
items.random_wrong: The Truman Doctrine ordered the dropping of the atomic bomb on Japan.
items.why: Containment was political and economic, not military invasion of the USSR. Students conflate Cold War rhetoric with hot war; Truman's doctrine was a framework for indirect rivalry.

items.q: Why did Stalin blockade West Berlin in 1948?
items.correct: To force the Western Allies out of Berlin in response to the merging of their zones, introduction of the Deutschmark, and creation of a separate West German state.
items.misconception: Stalin blockaded Berlin because he wanted to invade Western Europe.
items.random_wrong: Stalin blockaded Berlin to stop Nazis escaping to the West.
items.why: The blockade was reactive, not a step toward invasion. Students see all Soviet actions as offensive; in fact Berlin was a defensive move against Western consolidation.

items.q: Whose 1946 'Long Telegram' shaped American policy toward the USSR?
items.why: Correct answer: Kennan. “Truman” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which interpretation blames Stalin most heavily for the Cold War?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Orthodox. “Revisionist” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly how many million Soviet citizens died in WWII?
items.why: Exact answer: 27. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
